did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a convolutional neural network (CNN), a deep learning method, to perform multiclass classification of dermoscopic skin cancer images (melanoma, nevi, basal cell carcinoma, etc.) and compared its performance against 112 dermatologists.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled 'Original Research' on the first page. It describes a comparative study where a convolutional neural network (CNN) was trained and tested on 11,444 dermoscopic images and compared against 112 dermatologists. The study presents original experimental results, including sensitivity, specificity, and statistical significance (p < 0.001), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Systematic outperformance of 112 dermatologists in multiclass skin cancer image classification by convolutional neural networks
brief_summary: This study evaluated a convolutional neural network (CNN) for multiclass classification of dermoscopic skin cancer images against 112 dermatologists from 13 German university hospitals. Using 11,444 dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive, primarily the HAM10000 dataset, the CNN was trained to distinguish between five diagnostic categories: actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, melanoma, and melanocytic nevi. Performance was assessed on a test set of 300 biopsy-verified images, comparing the algorithm's sensitivity and specificity to the dermatologists' classifications. The CNN significantly outperformed the dermatologists in both binary (benign vs. malignant) and multiclass classification tasks, achieving higher specificity at equal sensitivity levels (p < 0.001). Specifically, the algorithm achieved a specificity of 91.3% for the primary endpoint and 98.8% for the secondary endpoint, compared to dermatologists' specificities of 59.8% and 89.2%, respectively. The findings demonstrate that automated multiclass classification can reflect clinical reality while systematically outperforming expert dermatologists.

lesion_types_studied.details: Grouped into a single compound class 'akiec' for the multiclass classification task. Mapped to malignant for binary classification.
One of the five diagnostic categories. Performance was on-par with dermatologists in multiclass classification.
Grouped into a single compound class 'bkl' for the multiclass classification task. Mapped to benign for binary classification.
One of the five diagnostic categories. Downsampled by a factor of 2 during training due to class imbalance.
One of the five diagnostic categories. Mapped to malignant for binary classification.

all_task_types.target: Dermoscopic skin cancer diagnostic categories (actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, melanoma, melanocytic nevus) and binary benign vs. malignant status.
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of dermoscopic images into five diagnostic categories (one-vs-all approach) and binary classification distinguishing benign from malignant lesions. The CNN predicts discrete diagnostic labels. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive, containing heterogeneous populations and images taken by different camera systems. Duplicates were removed by lesion ID so that only one image per lesion was used.

use.training: Used to train the CNN. The training set consisted of 11,394 images (after removing 50 test images). Due to class imbalance, nv was downsampled by a factor of 2, and others were upsampled by a factor of 3, resulting in a final training set of 12,336 images (585 akiec, 910 bcc, 3101 bkl, 4219 nv, 3521 mel).
use.testing: A subset of 300 biopsy-verified images (60 for each of the five disease classes) was used as the test set to evaluate the algorithm's performance against dermatologists.
use.other: The dataset was also supplemented with an additional 4291 images from the ISIC archive to ensure a sufficiently large number of images per skin disease.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: An external test set from a different dermoscopic image database used in a preliminary study to assess generalizability.

use.training: Used for training the last fully-connected layers of a binary-classification CNN in a preliminary study to calibrate the model to new dermatoscopes.
use.other: Used to demonstrate that deep learning algorithms can be calibrated to new dermatoscopes with relatively few images.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the sensitivity and specificity of the CNN against the dermatologists for binary and multiclass classification tasks, using 2x2 tables.
To adjust the significance threshold for multiple comparisons in the multiclass classification task (15 separate tests).
To estimate the uncertainty of sensitivity and specificity metrics for dermatologists and the CNN.

statistical_methods_used.method: Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves and Area Under the Curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the diagnostic performance of the CNN and dermatologists across different thresholds.
To binarize multiclass classification results for calculating sensitivity and specificity for each individual class against the rest.
To calculate the overall sensitivity and specificity for the multiclass classification task by averaging metrics over all five classes.
To combine dermatologists' numeric estimates of benignancy/malignancy and to determine the majority decision for differential diagnosis questions.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in sensitivity or specificity between the convolutional neural network (CNN) and the dermatologists for the classification tasks.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in sensitivity or specificity between the CNN and the dermatologists, specifically that the CNN outperforms the dermatologists.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was defined as p < 0.05. For the multiclass classification task, the alpha threshold was adjusted using Bonferroni correction to account for multiple comparisons (15 separate tests). The CNN significantly outperformed dermatologists in the primary endpoint (binary classification) with p < 0.001 and in the secondary endpoint (multiclass classification) with p < 0.001, except for basal cell carcinoma where performance was on-par.

HAM10000_Test_Set.description: Test set of 300 biopsy-verified images (60 for each of the five disease classes) from the HAM10000 dataset.

Binary_Classification_Primary_Endpoint.notes: Statistical significance for primary endpoint (binary classification) p < 0.001.

Multiclass_Classification_Secondary_Endp.notes: Statistical significance for secondary endpoint (multiclass classification) p < 0.001, except for basal cell carcinoma (bcc) where performance was on-par.

Class_Specific_Performance_One_vs_All.notes: Comparison of dermatologists' overall specificity to the classifier's specificity at equal sensitivity for each class. Outperformance achieved for all classes except bcc in the multiclass task.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly stated in the provided text, though ResNet50 is typically pretrained on ImageNet; the paper notes it was chosen based on 'good previous experience' and confirmation by Kassani and Kassani [15].

architecture_details.rationale: Chosen based on good previous experience with skin lesion classification and quantitative confirmation by Kassani and Kassani [15].

training_details.data_augmentation: Upsampling by a factor of 3 for classes other than nv; nv downsampled by a factor of 2.
training_details.final_training_set_size: 12,336 images (585 akiec, 910 bcc, 3101 bkl, 4219 nv, 3521 mel)

classification_or_output_head.details: For the main study, a single CNN was trained for each questionnaire. In a preliminary study for generalizability, the last fully-connected layers were trained on 100 images from the PH2 database.

preprocessing_techniques: Class balancing via upsampling (factor of 3) and downsampling (factor of 2) of the training set to address class imbalance
Data augmentation (specifically mentioned as the method for upsampling, though specific techniques like rotation/flip are not detailed)
Image quality filtering: Removal of 75 images from the test set that scored above a quality threshold of 2.5 (indicating insufficient or inadequate image quality)
Conversion of categorical image quality answers into integer grades (1-5) for dermatologist evaluation

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Class balancing via upsampling and downsampling
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Due to class imbalance, the 'nv' (melanocytic nevi) class was downsampled by a factor of 2, while other classes (akiec, bcc, bkl, mel) were upsampled by a factor of 3 via data augmentation. This resulted in a final training set of 12,336 images (585 akiec, 910 bcc, 3101 bkl, 4219 nv, 3521 mel).

conclusions: Automated multiclass classification of dermoscopic skin cancer images extends the capability of binary classification to better reflect clinical differential diagnoses while systematically outperforming dermatologists.
The convolutional neural network (CNN) significantly outperformed 112 dermatologists in both binary (benign vs. malignant) and multiclass classification tasks (p < 0.001).
In the primary endpoint (binary classification), the CNN achieved a specificity of 91.3% at equal sensitivity, compared to dermatologists' specificity of 59.8%.
In the secondary endpoint (multiclass classification), the CNN achieved a specificity of 98.8% at equal sensitivity, compared to dermatologists' mean specificity of 89.2%.

The CNN outperformed dermatologists for all five diagnostic classes (actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, melanoma, melanocytic nevi) except for basal cell carcinoma, where performance was on-par.
Deep learning algorithms can be calibrated to new dermatoscopes or preprocessing methods with relatively few images (e.g., 100 images) to restore performance.

The study acknowledges limitations including the use of only biopsy-verified images (introducing a bias towards difficult-to-diagnose lesions) and the lack of additional clinical information (e.g., patient history, palpation) available to dermatologists during clinical exams.
important_remarks: The study uses only biopsy-verified images for the test set, which introduces a selection bias towards lesions that are difficult to diagnose, potentially inflating the perceived difficulty for dermatologists and the relative performance of the CNN.

The test images originate from the same overall dataset (ISIC) as the training data, raising concerns about generalizability to truly external, out-of-distribution populations; however, a preliminary study showed performance could be restored on an external PH2 dataset with only 100 calibration images.

Dermatologists were evaluated based exclusively on dermoscopic images without access to additional clinical information (e.g., patient history, palpation, age) that is typically available during real-world clinical examinations, creating an unfair comparison against the algorithm.

The study relies on open-source data to ensure reproducibility, but acknowledges that supplementing with proprietary data could improve classifier performance, limiting the generalizability of the specific results to closed datasets.
Anonymity of the dermatologist survey carries a risk of abuse or carelessly provided answers, though this was mitigated by using institutional emails and majority voting.
Chief physicians' answers were omitted from the hierarchical analysis due to insufficient sample size, limiting the granularity of the expert comparison.
The CNN significantly outperformed dermatologists in binary classification (p < 0.001) and multiclass classification (p < 0.001), except for basal cell carcinoma where performance was on-par.
The study demonstrates that deep learning algorithms can be calibrated to new dermatoscopes or preprocessing methods with relatively few images (e.g., 100 images), suggesting high adaptability.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized the ISIC archive, primarily the HAM10000 dataset, for training and internal testing. A total of 11,444 dermoscopic images were used, with 11,394 images forming the training set (after removing 50 test images) and 300 biopsy-verified images forming the test set. The training set was balanced via upsampling (factor of 3) and downsampling (factor of 2). The test set consisted of 60 images per class for five diagnostic categories. The study also referenced a preliminary study involving the PH2 dataset to demonstrate calibration capabilities, but the main CNN evaluation was performed on the ISIC/HAM10000 test set.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The main study did not perform cross-dataset validation. The test set (300 images) was drawn from the same source (ISIC/HAM10000) as the training set. The paper mentions a preliminary study where a model trained on ISIC was tested on the PH2 dataset, but this was a separate experiment to demonstrate calibration, not part of the primary evaluation protocol for the 112 dermatologist comparison.

training.notes: Used to train the ResNet50 CNN. The dataset was supplemented with 4,291 additional images from ISIC. Class imbalance was addressed by downsampling 'nv' by factor 2 and upsampling other classes by factor 3.

validation.notes: The paper does not explicitly report a separate validation set used for model selection or hyperparameter tuning. It states the training and test sets were made disjunctive.

testing.notes: Used for the primary evaluation against 112 dermatologists. Images were biopsy-verified. 75 images were excluded due to low quality (score > 2.5), leaving 225 images for some analyses, though the primary comparison often cites the full 300 or the subset depending on the specific metric context in the text (e.g., Fig 1/2 analysis). The text states 'A subset of 75 images was taken out... because they scored above a certain quality threshold'. However, the abstract and results often refer to the 300 image set. The JSON extraction notes 300 test images.

external_testing.notes: Used in a preliminary study to demonstrate that a CNN trained on ISIC could be calibrated to a new dermatoscope/source with few images. Not used for the main dermatologist comparison.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: The study used a ResNet50 architecture, which is typically pretrained on ImageNet, but the paper does not explicitly detail the pretraining source or weights used, only that ResNet50 was chosen based on previous experience.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Data augmentation was used to upsample minority classes (akiec, bcc, bkl, mel) by a factor of 3. 'nv' was downsampled by a factor of 2.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: Training set: 11,394 images. Test set: 300 images (60 per class). The sets were disjunctive. 75 test images were excluded due to quality threshold > 2.5 for some analyses.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: The test set was drawn from the same ISIC archive as the training set. The paper acknowledges this raises concerns about generalizability to truly external test sets. A preliminary study used the PH2 dataset as an external test set for calibration purposes.

metrics.sensitivity: null (reported as equal sensitivity for specificity comparison)
metrics.specificity: 91.3% (binary, at equal sensitivity), 98.8% (multiclass, at equal sensitivity)

dataset_level_results.notes: CNN significantly outperformed dermatologists in both binary (p < 0.001) and multiclass (p < 0.001) tasks. Specificity reported at equal sensitivity.

other_metrics.performance_note: Performance dropped on external set but was completely restored after fine-tuning with 100 images.

dataset_level_results.notes: Used to demonstrate calibration capability. Not part of the main 112 dermatologist comparison.

results.value: Dropped initially, restored after fine-tuning with 100 images

cross_dataset_experiments.notes: This was a preliminary study mentioned in the discussion/limitations to address generalizability concerns. It is not the primary experiment of the paper.

generalizability_notes: The test set was drawn from the same ISIC archive as the training set, raising concerns about generalizability to truly external, out-of-distribution populations.
The study used only biopsy-verified images, which may introduce a selection bias towards difficult-to-diagnose lesions.
Dermatologists were evaluated on images only, without clinical context (history, palpation), creating an unfair comparison.
The paper acknowledges that supplementing with proprietary data could improve performance, limiting generalizability of specific results to open-source contexts.
information_not_reported: Explicit validation set size or role for model selection.
Specific data augmentation techniques (only factors mentioned).
Detailed pretraining weights source (only ResNet50 architecture mentioned).
